title: Fantasy marbling, freeform stylus work on a carrageenan size
subtitle: Painting directly on the size surface with a stylus for unrepeated patterns
excerpt: Fantasy marbling replaces the comb with a stylus or needle moved in spontaneous curves and spirals across the pigment on the size surface. Each sheet is unique. The technique is the most accessible entry point to marbling because it requires no combing technique and no pattern memorisation.
sourceNotes: Josef Halfer, The Progress of the Marbling Art (1893, English translation 1894), public domain. Halfer includes freeform stylus work in his repertoire of marbling methods, distinguishing it from the combed pattern traditions.

glossaryTerms.definition: A pointed tool used to draw through pigments on a marbling size. In marbling, any pointed implement: a wooden skewer, a turkey-baster tip, a long needle: serves as a stylus.

content.text: Fantasy marbling requires only that the maker drop colours on the size and draw through them with a

. The shapes the stylus creates: spirals, serpentine curves, concentric arcs, radiating lines: are entirely the maker's choice. No pattern vocabulary needs to be memorised. It is a good starting point for working out colour relationships and understanding how pigment behaves on the size before tackling the more controlled combed patterns.

attrs.heading: Safety: marbling chemistry
attrs.body: Carrageenan size and liquid acrylic pigments are non-toxic at marbling concentrations. Dispose of used size by diluting before draining. Alum mordanting solution irritates eyes; keep away from face and wash hands after use.

items.name: Carrageenan size (slug: carrageenan)
items.description: Prepared the day before at 1 teaspoon per litre. Rested until clear.

items.name: Marbling acrylic in 3-5 colours (slug: marbling-acrylic-liquid)
items.description: Each colour prepared with 1-2 drops of ox-gall per 30 ml to control spread on the size.

items.name: Marbling stylus (slug: marbling-stylus)
items.description: A pointed wooden skewer or darning needle. Fantasy work benefits from a finer point than the wide-tipped combs.

content.text: Drop colours onto the size surface using the eyedropper: let them fall from 5-8 cm above the surface so they spread naturally. Alternate colours so they sit adjacent rather than layered. Drop slowly enough that each colour spreads before the next lands on top of it. Fantasy work looks better with fewer, larger colour areas than with many small drops; aim for 4-6 drops per colour across the tray.

Place the stylus just below the surface of the pigment (not pressing into the size below) and draw it through the colours in a single continuous movement. Lifting and re-entering mid-pattern leaves a gap; work in long, continuous strokes. The stylus leaves a trail of the background colour wherever it passes through pigment areas.

Fantasy work uses free movement across the size. Options include: outward spirals, parallel serpentine curves, radial lines from a central point, and concentric closed curves. Move slowly throughout. The pigment must trail the stylus cleanly rather than tearing.

When the pattern is complete, lower the mordanted paper face-down and leave for 30-40 seconds. Lift by one corner, rinse under running water, and dry flat. The rinse is brief: hold the sheet under a gentle stream from the tap for 5-10 seconds; over-rinsing softens the alum mordant and lets colours bleed.
